q: When measuring crutch length, there should be how many finger widths between the pad and the anterior axillary fold?
rationale: The crutch length is measured with 2 to 3 finger widths between the pad and the anterior axillary fold. The tip is placed 6 inches lateral and 6 inches in front of the foot.

q: A patient is first day post-op right knee surgery with partial weight bearing allowed. Which gait should the nurse teach?
rationale: One leg affected = odd-numbered gait. Post-op with partial weight bearing = 3-point gait (2 crutches and bad leg move together, followed by the good leg). 3-1, 3-1, 3-1.

q: A patient has advanced-stage ALS with bilateral leg weakness. Which crutch gait is most appropriate?
rationale: Bilateral leg weakness = both legs affected = even-numbered gait. Advanced stages = severe = 4-point gait (move everything separately). Very slow but very stable.

q: When going up stairs with crutches, which moves first?
options: The bad foot goes up first
The good foot goes up first
rationale: Going UP stairs: good foot moves up first. Going DOWN stairs: bad foot moves down first. Both crutches always move with the bad leg.

q: A patient with a left above-the-knee amputation 2 days ago should use which gait?
rationale: An amputee is non-weight bearing. Swing-through gait is for non-weight bearing patients. It is similar to 3-point gait but the unaffected foot swings past the tip of both crutches.

q: A cane should be held on which side of the body?
rationale: Hold the cane on the unaffected (strong) side. Advance the cane with the opposite side for a wide base of support. The handgrip should be at the level of the wrist.

q: The correct way to use a walker is:
options: Pick it up, set it down, walk to it
Lean heavily on it while dragging feet
Swing both legs through the walker
rationale: The correct method: Pick up the walker, Set it down, Walk to it. The patient should have all four legs of the walker on the floor before walking to it.

q: A patient says, 'The CIA has implanted a tracking device in my brain.' This is an example of:
rationale: A delusion is a false, fixed belief with no sensory component - it's just a thought. A paranoid delusion involves believing people are out to get you or harm you.

q: A patient looks at the fire extinguisher on the wall and says, 'I see a bomb.' This is an example of:
rationale: An illusion involves a misinterpretation of a REAL stimulus. The fire extinguisher is real (the referent), but the patient misinterprets it as a bomb. A hallucination has NO real stimulus.

q: A patient with a blank stare says, 'I see a bomb.' There is nothing on the wall. This is:
rationale: A hallucination is a false sensory perception with NO real referent (stimulus). The patient sees something that is not actually there. In an illusion, there IS an actual object being misperceived.

q: For a patient with functional psychosis, the nurse should use which approach?
options: Redirect the patient to a new activity
Acknowledge feeling, present reality, set limits, enforce limits
rationale: Functional psychosis (schizophrenia, mood disorders): (1) Acknowledge feeling, (2) Present reality, (3) Set limits, (4) Enforce limits. These patients have the potential to learn reality.

q: The nurse is caring for a patient with psychosis of dementia. Which approach is most appropriate?
rationale: For psychosis of dementia: (1) Acknowledge their feeling, (2) Redirect them - give them something they can do. Dementia hallmark: memory loss, inability to learn. 2nd step begins with 'Re.'

q: Psychosis of delirium is best described as:
options: A permanent, progressive loss of reality
A temporary, sudden, dramatic loss of reality usually due to chemical imbalance
A learned behavior that can be unlearned
rationale: Delirium is temporary, sudden, dramatic, episodic, secondary loss of reality. Causes include UTI, thyroid imbalance, adrenal crisis, electrolytes, medications. Treat the underlying cause.

q: The handgrip measurement for crutches requires the angle of elbow flexion to be:
rationale: For proper crutch fitting, the angle of elbow flexion should be 30 degrees. The wrists should be at the level of the handgrip.

q: A non-psychotic patient is best approached using:
rationale: The non-psychotic patient has insight and is reality-based. Address them as you would any Med/Surg patient using therapeutic communication: reflection, clarification, amplification, restatement.

q: Which gait is described as 'very slow but very stable' where everything moves separately?
rationale: 4-point gait: move everything separately. Right crutch → Left foot → Left crutch → Right foot. Very slow but very stable. Used for bilateral leg weakness (even-numbered gait).

q: A patient with early-stage MS with bilateral mild leg weakness should use which gait?
rationale: Bilateral weakness = even-numbered gait. Early stage = mild. So use 2-point gait (milder, faster than 4-point). 2-point gait moves crutch and opposite foot at the same time.

q: Three types of delusions include:
rationale: Three types of delusions: Paranoid (people are out to get me), Grandiose (I'm the president), and Somatic (false belief about one's body).

q: When caring for a patient with delirium who has lost touch with reality, the nurse should:
options: Acknowledge feeling and reassure about safety and temporariness
rationale: For delirium: (1) Acknowledge feeling, (2) Reassure about safety and temporariness of condition. Do NOT try to redirect or teach reality - they have lost touch with reality.

q: The 2-point gait involves moving:
options: Both crutches and both legs separately in sequence
One crutch and the opposite foot at the same time
Both crutches together, then both feet
rationale: The 2-point gait moves one crutch and the opposite foot simultaneously. It is faster than 4-point gait and used for mild bilateral weakness.
